title: Morphodynamic study of hematopoietic stem cell emergence using shape matching methods with mechanical constraints
abstract: We developed geometry-aware methods for analyzing biological membrane morphology during hematopoietic stem cell emergence. Our approach integrates Helfrich energy constraints into shape-matching frameworks to quantify curvature dynamics in developmental processes.

title: not-MIWAE and a supervised MNAR extension
abstract: We extend the MIWAE framework for missing-not-at-random data imputation by incorporating supervised learning for censored observations. Our method leverages variational inference to handle complex missingness patterns in high-dimensional datasets.

title: Wasserstein-based methods for optimal transport in high-dimensional spaces
venue: Technical Report, MVA Geometric Data Analysis
abstract: This report reviews the 2011 paper by Rabin, Peyre, Delon, and Bernot on Wasserstein barycenters and texture mixing. We reproduce the texture synthesis experiments and provide a more efficient implementation to improve runtime and reproducibility.

title: Clairvoyance: An assistive vision system for smart glasses
abstract: We present Clairvoyance, an assistive computer vision pipeline for wearable smart glasses combining depth estimation, object detection and classification, and OCR for real-time scene understanding. The system implements a two-stage agentic workflow with custom-trained language models for planned scenarios and LLM-based fallback for unplanned requests.

title: Multimodal 3D Generation: Coupling Geometric Inductive Bias with 2D Foundation Priors
abstract: This survey compiles and structures the literature review conducted during my research collaboration initiation at NAIST Cybernetics and Reality Engineering Lab, under the supervision of Prof. Hideaki Uchiyama. In this review, I argue that the recent success of text- and image-conditioned 3D generation did not mainly come from scaling native 3D supervision, but from coupling geometric inductive bias with strong 2D foundation priors. Classical and early deep 3D methods were constrained by irregular geometry, partial observations, and limited 3D datasets.
